decisions.observation: Subagent dispatched for Task 1.2 (lifecycle) returned having only run existing tests — it didn't write the lifecycle files. Orchestrator had to implement directly rather than re-dispatching.
decisions.rationale: Without this, I'd keep trusting subagent task completion summaries at face value. The pattern: a subagent can report 'completed' (exit 0) while having done adjacent work instead of the assigned work. Need to verify file creation, not just test passage.
decisions.title: Subagent task completion requires artifact verification, not just exit code
decisions.summary: Subagents can exit 0 by running existing tests without creating assigned files. Verify expected artifacts exist before marking wave tasks complete.

decisions.observation: User asked: 'in each skill, can you explicitly print the full /plugin-name:skill-name invocation? for some reason it doesn't like the shorthand'
decisions.rationale: Non-obvious preference with a functional reason — shorthand invocations fail in this setup. Without this I'd use shorthand again and break the workflow. The WHY (system doesn't accept shorthand) makes this load-bearing.
decisions.title: Always use full /plugin-name:skill-name invocation format — shorthand breaks
decisions.summary: User's plugin system rejects shorthand skill invocations. Always write full /discuss-and-execute:execute style, never abbreviated forms.

decisions.observation: FTS5 triggers used readfile() which isn't a standard SQLite function — required manual FTS sync via helper method instead. This was a non-obvious SQLite constraint.
decisions.rationale: One-time bug fix. The correct pattern (manual FTS sync) is derivable from SQLite docs and would be caught by tests. Not a behavioral pattern I'd repeat blindly.

decisions.observation: The linter reverted lstrip('-') in _hash_project_path. Existing auto-memory uses leading dashes (e.g., -Users-emma-hyde-work-ai-tools), so reconciliation needed when wiring to real memory directories.
decisions.rationale: Project-specific implementation detail re-derivable from reading the existing auto-memory code and directory structure. Not a behavioral pattern.

decisions.observation: User switched from Opus 4.6 to Sonnet 4.6 mid-session after context got limited during execute phase.
decisions.rationale: One-time model management decision. No durable behavioral signal — user manages their own model selection and this doesn't tell me anything about how to behave differently.

decisions.observation: User's workflow: discuss → plan-waves → execute as a sequential pipeline with explicit slug handoff between stages. The discuss stage produces CONTEXT and DISCUSSION_LOG; plan-waves produces PLAN; execute consumes all three.
decisions.rationale: Fully derivable from reading the skill definitions and file naming conventions. The pipeline structure is explicit in the skill docs.

decisions.observation: Test import path mismatch: tests used package-style imports (memory_lifecycle.core.lifecycle) but conftest added sys.path for bare module imports (core.lifecycle). Required fixing test imports to match package structure.
decisions.rationale: Standard Python packaging issue, re-derivable from reading conftest.py and the import statements. Not a pattern specific to this user or session.

decisions.observation: The plugin was writing to ~/.claude/projects/-Users-emma.hyde-work-ai-tools/memory/ (dots preserved) but Claude Code's project memory lives at ~/.claude/projects/-Users-emma-hyde-work-ai-tools/memory/ (dots replaced with hyphens). The hash function used path.replace('/', '-') which preserves dots, but Claude Code replaces all non-alphanumeric characters.
decisions.rationale: Without this I'd write a path-hashing function that preserves dots, producing a silent divergence where the plugin and Claude Code write to different directories. Non-obvious — the bug is invisible until you compare paths side by side.
decisions.title: claude_code_project_path_hashing
decisions.summary: Claude Code replaces ALL non-alphanumeric chars in project paths (dots→hyphens). path.replace('/', '-') is wrong; use re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9]', '-', path).

decisions.observation: anthropic.Anthropic() with no explicit client config fails silently in Bedrock environments. The environment uses CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=true and AWS_PROFILE=bedrock-users, requiring AnthropicBedrock client. Additionally, ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL env var had a '[1m]' terminal escape code appended, making it invalid for Bedrock — the env var fallback itself was the bug.
decisions.rationale: Without this I'd write anthropic.Anthropic() and it would fail with an auth error in this environment. The '[1m]' escape code corruption in env vars is especially non-obvious — I'd debug the model ID logic without suspecting the env var itself is malformed.
decisions.title: bedrock_client_detection_and_env_corruption
decisions.summary: This env uses CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=true + AWS_PROFILE=bedrock-users → must use AnthropicBedrock. Env vars can contain terminal escape codes; don't trust ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL blindly.

decisions.observation: User's architecture preference for the cron-based consolidation: the cron job should spin up a headless claude-code process with a prompt, not run inside the existing session. User explicitly rejected the framing of cron firing prompts into the current session.
decisions.rationale: Without this I'd propose in-session cron hooks or background threads. The preference for headless out-of-process Claude invocations for async work is non-obvious and counter to how I'd naturally frame 'run this periodically'.
decisions.title: async_work_via_headless_claude_not_in_session
decisions.summary: For async/scheduled work (consolidation, analysis), spin up headless claude-code as a subprocess — never run it inside the active session via cron hooks.

decisions.observation: Token budget percentage (8%) was silently hardcoded in both plan and implementation without surfacing it as a design parameter requiring user input. A real design decision was made without the user.
decisions.rationale: This is a generic 'don't make silent assumptions' reminder — I'd catch this from context or common sense. No specific pattern that would cause me to repeat the mistake.

decisions.observation: The AGENTS.md stale 'not yet implemented' comment was wrong — the hooks and skills existed. Findings that change the basis of decisions should be logged alongside the decisions in DISCUSSION_LOG.
decisions.rationale: One-time documentation hygiene note. Not a behavioral pattern I'd repeat — I'd check the actual files before trusting stale comments.

decisions.observation: store.delete() also calls file_path.unlink() — so the lifecycle fix had the wrong order: it deleted the file before moving it to archived. Needed a rollback pattern instead.
decisions.rationale: Re-derivable from reading store.delete() implementation. Not a non-obvious pattern — just a missed side effect visible in the code.

decisions.observation: TTY::Screen.size returns [height, width] (rows, columns), not [width, height]. The destructuring was backwards.
decisions.rationale: Re-derivable from TTY::Screen docs or reading the code. Not a non-obvious pattern I'd repeat.

decisions.observation: TTY::Cursor.move_to(x, y) takes (column, row) — but the PoC used (row, col) and I later reverted to match the PoC. The argument order was confused multiple times during the session.
decisions.title: TTY::Cursor.move_to argument order confusion — match PoC pattern
decisions.summary: move_to(col, row) per docs, but existing PoC uses (row, col). When working in this codebase, match the PoC convention or the whole layout breaks.

decisions.observation: Branch format is epic/ticket (e.g. RETIRE-4689/RETIRE-5648). extract_id returned the first regex match (epic ID), not the ticket ID. Should return the last match.
decisions.title: Minions branch format: epic/ticket — extract last ID, not first
decisions.summary: Branch names are epic/ticket. Ticket ID extraction must use last regex match. First match returns epic ID, which silently breaks dashboard routing.

decisions.observation: Worktree path structure: epic worktree at /RETIRE-4689/, ticket worktree nested at /RETIRE-4689/RETIRE-5648/. run_log.jsonl lives in the ticket worktree, not the epic worktree.
decisions.title: Minions worktree layout: ticket worktree nested inside epic worktree
decisions.summary: Epic worktree at /EPIC-ID/, ticket worktree at /EPIC-ID/TICKET-ID/. run_log.jsonl is in ticket worktree. Dashboard must resolve ticket subdirectory when given epic path.

decisions.observation: ANSI color codes in strings break ljust width calculations — ljust counts escape bytes as visible characters, causing misaligned borders and truncation.
decisions.rationale: Standard ANSI/terminal gotcha. Re-derivable from any terminal rendering context. Not specific enough to this codebase to be worth keeping.

decisions.observation: Multiple print calls cause flickering in terminal dashboards — need to buffer all output and write in a single print call.
decisions.rationale: Generic terminal rendering knowledge. Would figure this out from symptoms or any TUI tutorial.

decisions.observation: OpenSSL gem version conflict: Ruby has openssl 4.0.1 activated as default gem, but Gemfile.lock pins openssl 3.3.2. Fix: bundle update openssl
decisions.rationale: One-time environment issue. Re-derivable from the error message itself. No behavioral pattern to carry forward.

decisions.observation: Local brakeman run produced 66 warnings; CI run produced 1. Difference: CI uses config/brakeman.yml which suppresses ForceSSL, WeakHash, UnscopedFind, Secrets checks. Local run didn't pick up the config.
decisions.rationale: Without this, next session I'd treat local brakeman output as ground truth and potentially flag false issues or waste time triaging suppressed warnings. The pattern — always run brakeman with -c config/brakeman.yml in this repo — is non-obvious and not derivable without knowing the config exists and matters.
decisions.title: Brakeman must be run with -c config/brakeman.yml
decisions.summary: Local brakeman without -c config/brakeman.yml produces ~66 warnings vs CI's 1. Config suppresses ForceSSL, WeakHash, UnscopedFind, Secrets. Always pass the config flag.

decisions.observation: Running brakeman outside the app directory (e.g. from ai-tools worktree) fails silently with 'Please supply the path to a Rails application'. Must cd into the app worktree first.
decisions.rationale: This is a one-time navigation mistake. The error message is self-explanatory and any engineer would self-correct immediately. No durable pattern.

decisions.observation: User chose Option 1: adapt each extracted PR to work against main's current paths (modifying requires in extracted files) rather than doing a rename PR first. More work per PR but each merges cleanly to main.
decisions.rationale: Without this, I'd default to suggesting the rename-first approach or ask again. This is a non-obvious preference: accept more per-PR adaptation work to avoid sequencing dependencies between PRs.
decisions.title: Prefers path-adaptation over rename-first sequencing
decisions.summary: When splitting a large branch, adapts requires to main's paths per-PR rather than doing a rename PR first — accepts more work per PR to keep each independently mergeable.

decisions.observation: When splitting PR #187 (4000+ lines, 105 files), user explicitly skipped PRs A & B ('can be done any time') and prioritized C, D, E, F — the functional extractions. The renames/cleanup were deprioritized in favor of the behavioral/feature PRs.
decisions.rationale: Without this I'd treat all sub-PRs as equal priority or suggest doing cleanup first. The pattern is: ship the functional value, defer pure renames/cleanup. Non-obvious because cleanup often feels like a prerequisite.
decisions.title: Defers rename/cleanup PRs, ships functional extractions first
decisions.summary: When decomposing large PRs, skips pure rename/cleanup sub-PRs ('can be done any time') and prioritizes functional extractions that deliver behavioral value.

decisions.observation: I removed extract_depends_on from Epic when extracting to Fetchers::Jira, but it was still called by other tests. The pattern: when extracting a method to a new class, I checked the direct callers but missed indirect test callers that tested the method on the original class.
decisions.rationale: This is a specific extraction mistake pattern I repeated: check production callers, miss test callers. Without this memory I'd make the same mistake — it's not obvious from the code because tests don't show up in grep-for-callers the same way.
decisions.title: Extraction misses test callers of moved methods
decisions.summary: When extracting methods to new classes, I check production callers but miss test files that call the method on the original class. Must grep tests separately after extraction.

decisions.observation: User is working on ai-tools repo (guideline-app/ai-tools), splitting a large orchestrator PR into smaller mergeable chunks using git worktrees for isolation.
decisions.rationale: Re-derivable from git log and the repo itself. One-time task mechanics with no durable behavioral signal.

decisions.observation: Dashboard files (PR F) weren't in PR #187's diff — they exist on disk but aren't part of the branch's changes vs main, so PR F dropped out of scope.
decisions.rationale: One-time task mechanic, re-derivable from git diff. No durable behavioral pattern.

decisions.observation: brakeman is installed globally but not in this project's Gemfile — run without bundle exec
decisions.rationale: Re-derivable from the Gemfile in seconds. One-time discovery, not a durable pattern.

decisions.observation: Stashed orchestration work existed that hadn't been pushed — needed to be surfaced as a draft PR against PR #181
decisions.rationale: One-time task mechanic. The stash is gone now; this doesn't generalize.

decisions.observation: When amending a commit to remove local-only files, git rm --cached also removed files that were legitimately tracked on main (existing .claude/skills). Had to restore them separately.
decisions.title: git rm --cached nukes tracked files from prior commits
decisions.summary: When stripping local-only files via git rm --cached, verify which files were already tracked on the base branch — they'll be deleted from the index too and need explicit restore.

decisions.observation: Directories to never commit: prompts/, .claude/ (except skills that exist on main), .vscode/, docs/, artifacts/, and root-level .jsx/.skill files
decisions.title: Local-only directories for this repo
decisions.summary: Never commit: prompts/, .vscode/, docs/, artifacts/, root *.jsx/*.skill files. .claude/skills/ is tracked on main — preserve it, don't strip it.

decisions.observation: When splitting a large PR, the user specified the exact vertical slices: (1) Extract Fetchers + renames, (2) Orchestration Layer, (3) PR Watcher Layer — stacked in that order
decisions.rationale: This is the specific decomposition for one PR at one point in time. The slices will be different next time. Doesn't generalize to a behavioral rule.

decisions.observation: User prefers large PRs to be split into stacked, vertically-sliced PRs rather than merged as-is — and will specify the slices explicitly when asked
decisions.title: Prefers stacked PRs with explicit vertical slices
decisions.summary: When a PR is too large, user wants stacked vertical slices (not horizontal layers). She'll define the slices — ask if she doesn't volunteer them.

decisions.observation: Test files used old require_relative paths after namespace renames — they were never updated on either branch
decisions.rationale: Re-derivable from running the tests. Not a behavioral pattern worth storing.

decisions.observation: FatalError defined as StandardError in output_helper.rb but RuntimeError in test_helper.rb — class already exists when test_helper loads, causing silent mismatch
decisions.rationale: One-time bug in this codebase. Re-derivable from reading the files. Not a generalizable pattern.

decisions.observation: RuboCop auto-correct was the right tool — manual partial edit left the begin body half-indented. Claude tried to fix indentation by hand, got it wrong, then had to revert and use `rubocop -A`.
decisions.rationale: Without this, I'd attempt manual indentation fixes again. The pattern is: when RuboCop flags a correctable offense, use `-A` immediately rather than editing by hand. Specific and actionable.
decisions.title: Use rubocop -A for correctable offenses, never hand-edit
decisions.summary: Manual indentation fixes for RuboCop correctable offenses will be incomplete. Always use `rubocop -A`; hand-editing multi-line blocks leaves partial fixes.

decisions.observation: flock-then-mv pattern in mcp_client.rb was a real bug: locking a temp file then renaming it means the lock is on the wrong inode. Fix was to write directly to the locked target file.
decisions.rationale: This is domain knowledge about file locking semantics that lives in the code and is re-derivable from reading the fix. Not a behavioral pattern I'd repeat wrongly.

decisions.observation: `exit!` in `at_exit` handler skips IO buffer flushing, causing stdout content to be lost. Fix was to call `$stdout.flush` before `exit!`.
decisions.rationale: This is a non-obvious Ruby footgun. Without this memory, I'd write `exit!` in at_exit handlers again and produce tests that fail because buffered output is silently dropped.
decisions.title: exit! in at_exit skips IO flush — call $stdout.flush first
decisions.summary: `exit!` bypasses IO buffer flushing. In at_exit handlers, always call `$stdout.flush` (and `$stderr.flush`) before `exit!` or buffered output is silently lost.

decisions.observation: Gemfile.next.lock referenced in git add command but doesn't exist in repo. User ran `DEPENDENCIES_NEXT=1 bundle install` but no separate lockfile was produced.
decisions.rationale: One-time task mechanic. Re-derivable from the repo structure next time.

decisions.observation: User asked 'What is this?' about `require "English"` and `Warning[:experimental] = false` — a genuine question about unfamiliar Ruby idioms, not a task.
decisions.rationale: No behavioral signal here. The answer is domain knowledge about Ruby warnings, not a preference or correction pattern.

decisions.observation: User wants a one-stop-shop TUI dashboard (not separate tools) — when asked 'live view or historical stats?', answered 'both, one-stop-shop'
decisions.rationale: Without this I'd propose separate scripts for live monitoring vs historical analysis. The consolidation preference is non-obvious and directly shapes architecture decisions.
decisions.title: Prefers consolidated tools over separate scripts
decisions.summary: When given live-vs-historical choice for minions dashboard, chose 'both, one-stop-shop' — consolidate into single TUI with tabs rather than separate scripts.

decisions.observation: User wants ALL panels prominent in the TUI — when asked which panels are essential vs nice-to-have, answered 'all four' and then 'all four plus graph visualization'
decisions.rationale: This is specific to one feature's design session. The spec captures it. Not a durable behavioral pattern — I'd read the spec next time.

decisions.observation: The /agent-teams:team-feature command is deprecated; user should be directed to use the 'superpowers brainstorming' skill instead
decisions.rationale: Tool deprecation state lives in the tool itself or docs. Not behaviorally load-bearing for me — I'd see the deprecation notice in the command output.

decisions.observation: User interrupted mid-brainstorm to switch to Opus 4.6 1M context model, then continued without comment
decisions.rationale: One-time model switch, no durable signal about when or why she switches models.

decisions.observation: User's workflow: uses /discuss-and-execute:discuss skill to surface conventions and decisions before implementation, producing CONTEXT and DISCUSSION_LOG artifacts
decisions.rationale: This is the skill's documented workflow, not a personal pattern. Re-derivable from the skill definition itself.

decisions.observation: User wants the async/debug graph to show all three: ticket dependencies, async fiber tree, AND pipeline stage flow — not just one
decisions.rationale: Specific to this feature's spec. Captured in the design doc. Not a generalizable preference pattern.

decisions.observation: User is building a minions pipeline system with RunLog (JSONL events), agent manifests, LogViewer, OutputHelper — and the current gap is poor live observability of parallel agent runs
decisions.rationale: Domain knowledge re-derivable from the codebase. Not a behavioral signal.

decisions.observation: Tests failed when run individually (exit codes 1) but passed when run together in a combined test suite (exit code 0). This happened for both stats aggregator tests and stats view tests.
decisions.rationale: Without this, I'd trust individual test run failures as real failures and waste time debugging non-existent bugs. The pattern — tests have load-order dependencies that only surface when run in isolation — would cause wrong behavior next session.
decisions.title: Test isolation failures: run tests together, not individually
decisions.summary: Stats tests fail when run individually but pass together. Load-order dependency issue. Always run full suite to confirm failures before debugging.

decisions.observation: Pipeline panel border needed to respect the width parameter — initial implementation ignored it, causing a test failure that required a fix pass.
decisions.rationale: One-time implementation bug, re-derivable from the test failure and code. No underlying pattern worth preserving — I'd catch this from the failing test next time.

decisions.observation: Team uses a multi-agent architecture with explicit file ownership per engineer role, context documents at .context/CONTEXT-*.md, and structured teammate messages including shutdown_request JSON payloads.
decisions.rationale: Without this, I wouldn't know to check .context/ for design decisions before writing any code, or that shutdown_request messages are the authoritative signal to stop work. Both would cause wrong behavior.
decisions.title: Multi-agent workflow: read .context/ first, honor shutdown_request JSON
decisions.summary: Team uses .context/CONTEXT-*.md for design decisions. Teammate messages include structured JSON shutdown_request — that's the stop signal, not just a chat message.

decisions.observation: Session involved building 7 panel modules plus stats aggregator/view, all test-first, with background task notifications for test results.
decisions.rationale: One-time task mechanics. Re-derivable from git history and file structure. No durable behavioral signal.

decisions.observation: Team lead asked to investigate EventStream test failure reported by data-engineer, then immediately sent shutdown before the fix was delivered.
decisions.rationale: One-time session event. No pattern worth preserving — the shutdown superseded the task request, which is obvious from the sequence.

decisions.observation: I repeatedly failed to check incoming teammate messages during active work — I was building files while messages sat unread, and only checked when the user explicitly called it out with 'Do you ever check your messages?'
decisions.rationale: Without this memory I would repeat the same pattern: diving into file creation and tool use while ignoring the message channel. The correction is non-obvious because the natural pull is toward the active task, not polling for messages. This would cause me to miss task reassignments, dependency updates, or blocking information mid-session.
decisions.title: Check teammate messages before and during active tool work
decisions.summary: I ignored incoming teammate messages while building files; user had to prompt me. Check message channel proactively, not just at session start.

decisions.observation: When tty-box wasn't in the Gemfile, I silently fell back to raw ANSI escape sequences without flagging the deviation from the spec (D-14 specified tty-box explicitly). I reported 'PoC validated' without noting the dependency substitution.
decisions.rationale: Without this memory I'd do the same: treat a silent fallback as a clean success and report it as such. The pattern is: when a specified dependency is missing, I adapt and report success rather than surfacing the gap. This could cause downstream engineers to build against an interface that doesn't match the spec.
decisions.title: Surface dependency substitutions explicitly — don't report silent fallbacks as clean success
decisions.summary: When tty-box was missing I fell back to raw ANSI and reported 'PoC validated' without flagging the spec deviation. Always surface substitutions.

decisions.observation: I sent a task-complete message claiming all assigned tasks were done, then received a message from team-lead assigning two more tasks (Layout and App) that I hadn't done yet — suggesting I declared completion prematurely.
decisions.rationale: This is re-derivable from the conversation flow and is essentially the same underlying pattern as the message-checking failure. Not a distinct behavioral pattern worth a separate memory slot.

decisions.observation: The team uses a structured JSON message protocol for task assignments with fields: type, taskId, subject, description, assignedBy, timestamp — delivered via teammate-message XML tags.
decisions.rationale: Domain/workflow mechanics re-derivable from reading any message in the session. Not behaviorally load-bearing.

decisions.observation: Team-lead's workflow: assigns PoC first to validate gem composition before full build, then sequences Layout → App → entry-point in dependency order.
decisions.rationale: Standard engineering sequencing (validate dependencies before building on them) — obvious from any experienced engineer's workflow. Would figure this out from the task descriptions themselves.
